= lab-03-apps-never-down (Web Console)

// Auto-generated console guide from playbook. Source: repos/zt-ocp4-getting-started/content/modules/ROOT/pages/lab-03-apps-never-down.adoc
// Generated: 2026-04-07T18:06:14.069Z
// Review and edit before publishing.

=== Student logs in to the OpenShift console and navigates to the Core Platform perspective to see their provisioned projects

Click the *OpenShift Console* tab on the right side of this lab interface.

Log in with your credentials:

* *Username*: `{user}`
* *Password*: `{password}`

After logging in, click the perspective switcher in the top-left corner — it may default to *Developer*. Select *Core platform*.

image::console-guide/01-core-platform-projects.png[Core Platform perspective showing student projects]

You will see 3 Projects: `{user}-datasphere`, `{user}-lab`, and `{user}-lab-helm`. Click *`{user}-datasphere`* to continue.

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/01-core-platform-projects.png[Core Platform perspective showing the 3 student projects: user-wzpnt-datasphere, user-wzpnt-lab, user-wzpnt-lab-helm]

=== Student finds the DataSphere app URL via the Routes view and opens the app in a new tab

In the left navigation, click *Networking* → *Routes*.

You will see the `datasphere-ui` route with status *Accepted*. Click the URL in the *Location* column to open the DataSphere app in a new browser tab.

image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/02-routes-datasphere-ui.png[Routes list showing the datasphere-ui route and its URL]

You should see the DataSphere map with all 30 datacenters showing green status.

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/02-routes-datasphere-ui.png[Routes list in user-wzpnt-datasphere project showing the datasphere-ui route with its URL]

=== Student navigates to Pods view to see the running UI pod before deleting it

In the left navigation, click *Workloads* → *Pods*.

You will see 3 pods running — the API, Redis, and UI. Note the *Restarts* column is 0 for all of them. Find the `datasphere-ui-*` pod.

image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/03-pods-list-all-running.png[Pods list showing all 3 pods running with 0 restarts]

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/03-pods-list-all-running.png[Pods list showing all 3 pods running: datasphere-api, datasphere-redis, and datasphere-ui, all with 1/1 Ready and 0 Restarts]

=== Student clicks into the datasphere-ui pod, opens the Actions menu, and selects Delete Pod to trigger an outage

In the Pods list, click the name of the `datasphere-ui-*` pod to open its detail page.

image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/04-ui-pod-details.png[Pod detail page showing Running status and the Actions button]

Click *Actions* → *Delete Pod*.

image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/05-actions-delete-pod.png[Actions dropdown showing the Delete Pod option]

A confirmation dialog appears showing the exact pod name and namespace.

image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/06-delete-pod-confirm.png[Delete Pod confirmation dialog]

Click *Delete*. Then switch to your browser tab with the DataSphere app — the page will go blank as your only UI pod is gone. Within a few seconds, OpenShift creates a replacement and the page comes back.

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/04-ui-pod-details.png[Pod details page for the datasphere-ui pod showing Running status, component=ui label, and the Actions button]

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/05-actions-delete-pod.png[Actions dropdown on the pod detail page showing the Delete Pod option]

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/06-delete-pod-confirm.png[Delete Pod confirmation dialog showing the pod name and namespace before confirming deletion]

=== Student watches the Pods view auto-refresh to show the old pod terminating and the new one already being created by the ReplicaSet

Switch back to the Pods list (*Workloads* → *Pods*). The console auto-refreshes — you will see the old pod in *Terminating* status while OpenShift has already created a replacement in *Container Creating*.

image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/07-pod-terminating-new-creating.png[Pods list showing old pod terminating and new pod already creating]

Within a few seconds the replacement pod reaches *Running* and the DataSphere app comes back. OpenShift replaced the pod automatically — but your users experienced a real outage during the gap. With only 1 replica, any pod failure is user-visible.

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/07-pod-terminating-new-creating.png[Pods list showing the old datasphere-ui pod Terminating while a new one is already Container Creating - OpenShift automatically started a replacement]

=== Student scales the datasphere-ui deployment to 3 replicas using the Edit Pod count dialog, then verifies all 3 are running

In the left navigation, click *Workloads* → *Deployments*, then click *datasphere-ui*.

On the deployment detail page, click *Actions* → *Edit Pod count*.

image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/09-deployment-actions-menu.png[Deployment Actions menu showing Edit Pod count and other options]

In the dialog, change the value to `3` and click *Save*.

image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/10-edit-pod-count-dialog.png[Edit Pod count dialog set to 3]

Click the *Pods* tab to watch all 3 replicas come up. Wait until all show `1/1` in the *Ready* column before continuing.

image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/12-deployment-3-pods-running.png[Pods tab showing all 3 datasphere-ui pods running 1/1]

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/08-deployment-ui-scale-control.png[datasphere-ui Deployment details page showing the pod count donut with up/down scaling arrows — currently showing 1 Pod]

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/09-deployment-actions-menu.png[Deployment Actions menu showing Edit Pod count, Add HorizontalPodAutoscaler, Edit Health Checks and other options]

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/10-edit-pod-count-dialog.png[Edit Pod count dialog showing the current count of 1 with +/- controls and a Save button]

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/11-edit-pod-count-3.png[Edit Pod count dialog with value changed to 3, ready to save]

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/12-deployment-3-pods-running.png[Pods tab of datasphere-ui deployment showing all 3 pods running 1/1 after scaling]

=== Student navigates to the HPA details page to see the current state before triggering load

Now let's look at the API — that's where the auto-scaler lives. In the left navigation, click *Workloads* → *HorizontalPodAutoscalers*.

You will see the `datasphere-api` HPA. Click it to open the details.

image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/13-hpa-details-top.png[HPA details showing min replicas 1, max replicas 5, current replicas 1]

This page shows the *Current replicas* and *Desired replicas* in real time. Keep this tab open — you will watch these numbers change when load climbs.

NOTE: For an even more visual view, open the `datasphere-api` Deployment (*Workloads* → *Deployments* → *datasphere-api*) and click the *Metrics* tab. The CPU usage graph will spike visibly as load increases.

image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/14-deployment-api-metrics.png[datasphere-api Deployment Metrics tab showing live CPU usage graph]

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/13-hpa-details-top.png[HPA details for datasphere-api showing min/max replicas, current replicas 1, desired replicas 1, and scale target]

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/14-deployment-api-metrics.png[datasphere-api Deployment Metrics tab showing live CPU usage, Memory usage, Filesystem and Network graphs]

=== Student drags the Simulated Traffic slider to 50% and watches the HPA scale up through the console

Switch to the DataSphere app browser tab and drag the *Simulated Traffic* slider to about *50%*.

Switch back to the console. On the HPA details page, watch the *Current replicas* and *Desired replicas* numbers climb as CPU crosses the 50% threshold. The *Last scale time* field will update when scaling occurs.

On the `datasphere-api` Deployment *Metrics* tab, watch the *CPU usage* graph spike upward — you are seeing the platform respond to real load in real time.

When you are done, drag the slider back to *0%*. Replicas will begin scaling down after about 20 seconds.

NOTE: The console auto-refreshes every 30 seconds. If you want a faster view, use the browser refresh button while watching the HPA details page.

=== Student triggers a health failure via the terminal and watches the pod restart in the console Pods view

Switch to the *Terminal* tab and run:

[source,role="execute",subs="attributes+"]
----
curl -s -X POST https://$(oc get route datasphere-ui -o jsonpath='{.spec.host}')/api/break
----

Switch back to the *OpenShift Console* tab. Go to *Workloads* → *Pods* and watch the `datasphere-api-*` pod. Within about 40 seconds you will see two things happen in sequence:

. The pod changes to *0/1* in the *Ready* column — the readiness probe pulls it from the Service
. The *Restarts* counter increments — the liveness probe restarted the container

The DataSphere map never went down: the other replicas kept serving requests while the broken pod was restarted.

TIP: Click on the API pod name, then click the *Events* tab to see the liveness probe failure events that triggered the restart.

=== Student deploys the full DataSphere stack by pasting pre-rendered YAML into the Import YAML editor

Switch to the `{user}-lab` Project using the project dropdown at the top of the page.

Click the *+* (Import YAML) button in the top navigation bar, or go to the URL: `{openshift_console_url}/k8s/ns/{user}-lab/import`

Paste the following YAML into the editor:

[source,yaml,role="copypaste"]
----
include::partial$datasphere-lab-manifests.yaml[]
----

Click *Create*. The console will show each resource being created in real time.

image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/17-import-yaml-datasphere.png[Import YAML editor with DataSphere manifests pasted in]

When all resources show a green *Created* status, your DataSphere stack is deployed.

image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/18-resources-creating.png[Resources successfully created showing all 8 DataSphere resources with green Created status]

NOTE: We deliberately excluded the auto-scaler, health probes, and resource requests from this manifest. You will configure each one yourself in the steps below.

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/15-switch-to-lab-project.png[Core Platform showing user-wzpnt-lab project selected with no pods — the clean namespace ready for deployment]

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/16-datasphere-helm-catalog.png[Helm Charts catalog filtered to DataSphere Lab Charts showing the Datasphere chart ready to install]

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/17-import-yaml-datasphere.png[Import YAML editor with the DataSphere manifests pasted in, showing syntax-highlighted YAML ready to create]

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/18-resources-creating.png[Creating resources screen showing all 8 DataSphere resources being created: ConfigMap, 3 Services, 3 Deployments, and a Route, all with Creating status]

=== Student sets CPU and memory resource requests and limits on the datasphere-api deployment

Go to *Workloads* → *Deployments* → *datasphere-api*. Click *Actions* → *Edit resource limits*.

image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/19-edit-resource-limits-form.png[Edit resource limits dialog showing CPU and Memory Request/Limit fields]

Set these values:

* *CPU Request*: `25` — change the unit dropdown to `m` (millicores)
* *CPU Limit*: `100` — unit `m`
* *Memory Request*: `64` — unit `Mi`
* *Memory Limit*: `128` — unit `Mi`

Click *Save*. The HPA needs these requests to have a baseline to measure CPU utilization against. Without resource requests, the HPA has nothing to compare against and cannot make scaling decisions.

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/19-edit-resource-limits-form.png[Edit resource limits dialog for the api container showing CPU Request/Limit and Memory Request/Limit fields with unit dropdowns]

=== Student adds a HorizontalPodAutoscaler to the datasphere-api deployment via the console form

Back on the `datasphere-api` Deployment details page, click *Actions* → *Add HorizontalPodAutoscaler*.

image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/20-add-hpa-form.png[Add HorizontalPodAutoscaler form showing Name, Minimum Pods, Maximum Pods and CPU Utilization fields]

NOTE: The warning at the top confirms why you set resource requests first — the HPA cannot measure CPU utilization without them.

Fill in the form:

* *Name*: `datasphere-api`
* *Minimum Pods*: `2`
* *Maximum Pods*: `5`
* *CPU Utilization*: `50`

Click *Save*. The HPA will begin watching the API's CPU utilization and scale between 2 and 5 replicas automatically.

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/20-add-hpa-form.png[Add HorizontalPodAutoscaler form showing Name, Minimum Pods, Maximum Pods, and CPU Utilization fields, with a warning that resource requests must be set first]

=== Student adds liveness and readiness probes to the datasphere-api deployment via the Add Health Checks form

Back on the `datasphere-api` Deployment details page, click *Actions* → *Add Health Checks*.

image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/21-add-health-checks.png[Add health checks page showing Readiness, Liveness, and Startup probe sections]

The page explains the difference between probe types. Click *+ Add Liveness probe*.

image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/22-liveness-probe-form.png[Liveness probe form with HTTP GET type selected]

Fill in the liveness probe:

* *Type*: `HTTP GET`
* *Path*: `/health`
* *Port*: `8080`
* *Initial delay*: `10`
* *Period*: `10`
* *Failure threshold*: `3`

Then click *+ Add Readiness probe* and fill in:

* *Type*: `HTTP GET`
* *Path*: `/health`
* *Port*: `8080`
* *Initial delay*: `5`
* *Period*: `5`

Click *Add*. Both probes are now configured. The liveness probe will restart the container if `/health` fails 3 times. The readiness probe will remove the pod from the Service until it recovers.

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/21-add-health-checks.png[Add health checks page showing Readiness probe, Liveness probe, and Startup probe sections with Add links and descriptions of each probe type]

+
image::console-guide/lab-03-apps-never-down/22-liveness-probe-form.png[Liveness probe form expanded showing Type HTTP GET dropdown, Use HTTPS checkbox, and HTTP Headers fields]
